## Appendix D: Additional information for screening criteria and radionuclide specific analysis
When testing drinking water for radionuclides, gross alpha and gross beta screening and radionuclide specific testing results cannot be compared. The general intent of the screening test is to apply a method that gives a simple, quick, reliable and inexpensive determination of whether more characterization is required. If the gross alpha and beta measurements are below their screening levels, no further testing or assessment is recommended. The screening levels were chosen to be well below any observable health consequences in all situations. If the gross alpha and beta measurements are above the screening levels, radionuclide-specific testing is recommended.
Radionuclide-specific testing and assessment can be more expensive than screening tests, take longer to perform, and radiation protection knowledge is needed to interpret the results.
While it seems logical that adding up the quantities from the radionuclide-specific testing would result in similar quantities as the gross alpha and beta measurements, or that these should at the very least be correlated, this is not the case in many instances. It is not uncommon to have a gross alpha or beta measurement above the detection limit or even above the screening criteria, with no exceedances in the activity concentration of individual radionuclides when radionuclide specific testing is performed. Detailed explanations can be found in the literature (Health Canada, 2021; Eaton and Shannon, 2015; Arndt and West, 2004; Welch et al, 1995).
Briefly, the reasons for this are:
- Uranium is a radionuclide that emits alpha and beta particles, but is not considered or tested as a radionuclide for drinking water. If uranium is present in the drinking water, this will result in an increased gross alpha measurement, and increased gross beta from short lived Pa-234 and Th-234. However, it is not recommended that uranium be tested as a radionuclide. This is because, even though natural uranium is weakly radioactive substance, the principal health effects are due to its chemical toxicity. Thus, it is recommended to test for uranium and compare the results to its Maximum Acceptable Concentration (MAC) which is based on its chemical toxicity (Health Canada, 2019a).
- Uranium is normally reported in total mass of uranium-238, uranium-235 and uranium-234 per litre for comparison to the chemical MAC. It is usually assumed that uranium-238, uranium-235 and uranium-234 are present in the same ratios as found in nature (natural uranium). However, in some groundwater cases, uranium-234 concentrations can be elevated compared to uranium-238, and this should be taken into consideration when attributing the contribution of uranium alpha activity to the gross alpha measurements.
- The time between collection of a sample and measurement could affect the results. Some of the radionuclides in the drinking water could decay away, and no longer be measured. Some radionuclides decay to others contributing to an increase in the gross alpha/beta measurement. For example, if a water sample containing radium-226 was stored for 30 days before testing, the radium-226 would decay to other radionuclides that were not present at the time of collection. This would lead to an elevated gross alpha measurement.
- Radionuclide-specific testing and screening methods. For radionuclide-specific measurement and for the gross alpha and beta screening measurement, specific instruments, setups and procedures are required. For either alpha or beta screening, one instrument, setup and measurement, is used to measure all the emission from all radionuclides present. Although this is effective as a screening tool, it cannot be used to determine the quantity of specific radionuclides. For the radionuclide-specific measurement, each instrument calibrated and optimized for the specific radionuclide's energy range. Both are measured and reported using the same units, Bq/l, but these are not equivalent.
